name: error-conversion-guide
description: Guides users on error conversion patterns, From trait implementations, and the ? operator. Activates when users need to convert between error types or handle multiple error types in a function.
capabilities: Detect error type mismatches in Rust code and suggest From trait implementations
Recommend #[from] attribute for automatic conversion instead of manual map_err calls
Show how to unify multiple error types into a single error enum
Provide patterns for layered architectures with domain and infrastructure errors
Guide on using thiserror for libraries and anyhow for applications
Explain when to use map_err versus implementing From
author: EmilLindfors
securitySummary: All static findings are false positives. The 42 'external_commands' detections are markdown code fences (triple backticks for Rust examples). The 'network' detection is a Rust method call (repository.fetch), not a JavaScript Fetch API. The 'blocker' findings are unfounded. This is a benign documentation skill with Rust code examples only.
valueStatement: Developers struggle with Rust error type mismatches and repetitive map_err calls. This skill provides idiomatic patterns for converting errors using From trait implementations, the ? operator, and libraries like thiserror and anyhow.
userTitle: Convert error types with From trait patterns
limitations: This skill provides guidance only; it does not modify code automatically
The skill does not analyze external crates or their error types
Pattern suggestions assume standard Rust error handling practices
Does not cover async error handling specifics

useCases.title: Migrating from manual error conversion to #[from]
useCases.description: A developer has repetitive map_err calls like .map_err(|e| AppError::Io(e))? and wants cleaner code. The skill shows how to use #[from] for automatic From implementation.

useCases.title: Creating unified error types for microservices
useCases.description: A team needs to combine database, API, and validation errors into one type. The skill demonstrates creating a unified error enum with From implementations for each source.

useCases.title: Choosing between thiserror and anyhow
useCases.description: A developer is unsure whether to use thiserror or anyhow for their project. The skill explains trade-offs: thiserror for libraries with specific error types, anyhow for applications needing flexibility.

promptTemplates.title: Convert map_err to From trait
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to simplify error conversion code

promptTemplates.title: Unify multiple error types
promptTemplates.prompt: My Rust function calls three different functions that return different error types (RepositoryError, ValidationError, ApiError). How can I make them all compatible with the ? operator?
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs to combine heterogeneous error types

promptTemplates.title: Add context during error conversion
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to add custom context while converting errors
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to convert an error but also add context about where the failure happened. What's the idiomatic way to do this in Rust? Should I use map_err, context, or implement From manually?

promptTemplates.title: Library versus application error handling
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm building a Rust library. Should I use thiserror or anyhow for my error types? What are the trade-offs for my library users?
promptTemplates.scenario: Developer choosing error handling strategy for a library

bestPractices: Use #[from] as the default pattern for automatic error conversion, reserving map_err for one-off conversions or when additional context is needed
Create a unified error type for your application that can convert from all the error types you encounter
Preserve the error chain by passing the error directly rather than converting to String, which loses source information
antiPatterns: Converting errors to String with map_err(|e| MyError::Failed(e.to_string())) loses the error chain and source information
Having multiple #[from] variants for the same source type causes compilation conflicts; use #[source] instead
Using anyhow in library code forces a dependency on users; prefer thiserror for libraries

faq.question: What is the difference between #[from] and #[source] in thiserror?
faq.answer: #[from] generates a From implementation for automatic conversion. #[source] stores the error as the cause but does not generate From. Use #[from] when you want ? to work. Use #[source] when you need multiple variants for the same error type.

faq.question: How do I add context to an error during conversion?
faq.answer: For one-off cases, use map_err with format!: .map_err(|e| MyError::Failed(format!("Operation failed: {}", e))). For anyhow, use .context("Failed to load config") instead.

faq.question: Can I implement From for types outside my crate?
faq.answer: No, you can only implement From for types you own. For external types, use map_err or wrap the error in your own type with #[from] on that wrapper.

faq.question: How do I handle errors from multiple crates in one function?
faq.answer: Create a unified error enum with #[from] on each variant that needs automatic conversion. Each source error type gets its own From implementation.

faq.question: Should I use Box<dyn Error> or a custom enum?
faq.answer: Use a custom enum when you need to handle specific errors differently. Use Box<dyn Error> when you need flexibility or are writing a library that must accept any error type.

faq.question: What is the question mark operator (?) doing with errors?
faq.answer: The ? operator calls Into::into on the error to convert it to the function's return type. This requires a From implementation. It also propagates the error early return.
